questions.question: What is the default display behavior of a <div> element?
questions.explanation: A <div> is a block-level element by default, meaning it takes up the full width available and starts on a new line.

questions.question: What is the default display behavior of a <span> element?
questions.explanation: A <span> is an inline element by default, meaning it only takes up as much width as its content and does not start on a new line.

questions.question: When should you use a <div> instead of a <span>?
choices: When you need to style a single word within a paragraph
When you need to group block-level content together
When you want to make text bold
When you need an inline container
questions.explanation: Use <div> to group block-level content (sections, layouts, containers). Use <span> for inline content like styling a word within a sentence.

questions.question: Which CSS property can change an inline element to behave like a block element?
questions.explanation: The display property controls how an element is rendered. Setting display: block on an inline element makes it behave like a block element.

questions.explanation:

) take up the full width of their parent container and always start on a new line.

questions.question: An ___ element only takes up as much width as its content needs.
questions.explanation: Inline elements (like <span>, <a>, <strong>) flow within text and only occupy the width of their content, without forcing a new line.
